answer: TEU is the standard container unit: one 20-foot container equals 1 TEU and one 40-foot container is usually counted as 2 TEU.
FEU stands for Forty-foot Equivalent Unit. One FEU is typically equal to 2 TEU.
BOR is the percentage of time berth capacity is occupied in a given period: berth time used divided by berth time available.
YOR is the proportion of yard storage capacity currently occupied. High YOR can increase re-handling and reduce yard efficiency.
Vessel turnaround time is total time from arrival to departure, including waiting, berthing, and cargo operations.
Dwell time is how long cargo or containers stay in terminal storage before gate-out or onward transfer.
Moves per hour measures container crane productivity and is calculated as total container moves divided by operating hours.
Truck turnaround time is elapsed time from truck entry to exit at the terminal, reflecting gate and yard efficiency.
LOA is the maximum vessel length from bow to stern used in berth and channel planning.
Beam is vessel width at its widest point and is important for channel width and maneuvering design.
Draft is vertical distance between waterline and keel. It defines minimum required depth for safe navigation.
Air draft is the height from waterline to highest point of a vessel and is critical near bridges or overhead structures.
DWT is the maximum weight a ship can safely carry, including cargo, fuel, water, and stores.
Gross tonnage is a non-weight index of vessel internal volume used for fees and regulations.
Net tonnage approximates cargo-carrying earning space and is used in some port charging systems.
UKC is safety distance between vessel keel and seabed, accounting for squat, tide, waves, and uncertainty.
Squat is dynamic sinkage and trim change of a moving vessel in shallow or confined water.
ETA is expected vessel arrival time used for berth planning and operation scheduling.
ETD is expected vessel departure time used for coordination with pilots, tugs, and subsequent berth allocation.
All fast means vessel mooring is completed and lines are secured at berth.
NOR is formal notice from vessel that it is ready to load or discharge cargo.
Laytime is contractually allowed time for cargo operations before demurrage applies.
Demurrage is penalty paid for exceeding agreed laytime in charter-party operations.
Despatch is reward paid when cargo operations finish earlier than allowed laytime.
Throughput is total cargo volume handled over a period, measured in TEU, tons, units, or cubic meters.
A breakwater protects harbor waters by reducing wave energy entering the basin.
Rubble mound breakwater uses layered rock armor, filter, and core materials for wave energy dissipation.
A caisson breakwater uses large concrete caissons placed on prepared seabed foundations.
Overtopping occurs when wave run-up passes over structure crest, potentially affecting safety and operations behind it.
Wave run-up is maximum vertical uprush of water on structure face above still water level.
Toe protection prevents scouring and instability at the base of breakwaters or revetments.
Scour is local seabed erosion around structures caused by currents, propeller wash, or waves.
Armor units are outer protective elements resisting wave forces on breakwaters.
Hudson formula estimates required armor stone size based on wave height, slope, and stability coefficient.
Van der Meer methods provide advanced armor stability relationships including wave period and damage level effects.
A quay wall is a berth structure retaining backfill and providing ship interface for cargo operations.
Gravity quay walls resist loads primarily through self-weight and base friction.
Sheet pile quay walls use steel or concrete piles with anchoring systems to resist lateral earth and berthing loads.
Pile-supported wharves transfer deck and operational loads through piles into deeper bearing strata.
Berthing energy is kinetic energy of vessel during approach that must be absorbed by fender system.
Fenders absorb vessel impact and limit reaction forces on both ship hull and berth structure.
Bollards are mooring fixtures on quay used to secure vessel lines.
Mooring dolphins are independent structures for mooring lines when quay length or geometry is limited.
Breasting dolphins take transverse berthing loads and can support loading platforms in some terminals.
Dolphin berths use separated mooring and breasting dolphins, common in liquid bulk terminals.
Revetments protect shoreline or embankments from wave and current erosion.
Seawalls are coastal defense structures designed to protect land against wave attack and flooding.
Navigation channels provide safe access path for vessels and are designed by width, depth, alignment, and aids to navigation.
Turning basins are widened areas that allow vessels to turn safely near berths or harbor entrances.
Harbor tranquility refers to acceptable wave conditions within basin for safe berthing and cargo operations.
Container terminals handle standardized containers using STS cranes, yard systems, and gate operations.
Dry bulk terminals handle loose solids such as coal, grain, and ore using unloaders, conveyors, and stockpiles.
Liquid bulk terminals handle fuels and chemicals through loading arms, pipelines, pumps, and tank farms.
General cargo terminals handle packaged, break-bulk, and project cargo with flexible equipment.
Ro-Ro terminals handle wheeled cargo via ramps, marshalling yards, and high-turnover traffic systems.
Multipurpose terminals handle mixed cargo types on shared infrastructure, improving flexibility but sometimes lowering peak productivity.
STS cranes transfer containers between vessel and terminal transport equipment at berth.
RTG cranes stack and retrieve containers in yard blocks with rubber-tire mobility.
RMG cranes run on fixed rails and are used for high-density or automated yard operations.
Reach stackers are mobile machines used for container handling in smaller yards and intermodal zones.
Straddle carriers move and stack containers directly, often used in high-speed terminal systems.
Terminal tractors move containers on chassis between quay and yard areas.
Stacker-reclaimers build and recover bulk stockpiles in dry bulk terminals.
Grab unloaders discharge bulk cargo from vessel holds, often feeding hoppers and conveyor systems.
Continuous unloaders provide high-rate bulk discharge with stable flow to conveyor networks.
Hoppers receive cargo from unloaders and regulate controlled transfer to downstream handling equipment.
Tank farms are clustered storage tanks for liquid cargo with safety, pumping, and containment systems.
Marine loading arms transfer liquids between shore and vessel with articulated joints and safety systems.
A manifold is a controlled pipeline connection hub for distribution among tanks, pumps, and berths.
Berth window is a scheduled time slot allocated to a vessel for berthing and operations.
Berth allocation is assignment of vessels to berths over time to balance service level and berth utilization.
A stowage plan defines cargo arrangement on vessel considering stability, sequence, and discharge ports.
Bay plan is a structural map of container slots used for planning vessel loading and unloading operations.
Reefer points are powered electrical sockets for refrigerated containers in terminal yards.
Empty depots store and reposition empty containers for shipping line and supply chain balancing.
Gateway ports connect maritime shipping with inland origin-destination cargo demand in their hinterland.
Transshipment hubs transfer containers between vessels and feeder services with minimal local final demand.
Hub-and-spoke networks route cargo through central hubs connected to regional feeder ports.
Feeder services connect smaller regional ports to mainline hubs in liner shipping networks.
Mainline services are long-haul shipping routes operated by larger vessels between major hubs.
Hinterland is inland region economically served by a port through logistics corridors.
A Port Community System is a digital platform for data exchange among terminal, customs, shipping lines, and logistics actors.
A TOS manages planning and real-time execution of berth, yard, gate, and equipment operations.
National single window systems streamline trade documentation and regulatory approvals across agencies.
Customs clearance is regulatory process for legal cargo import/export release with duties and compliance checks.
Incoterms define buyer-seller responsibilities for transport cost, risk transfer, and delivery points in trade contracts.
Bill of Lading is transport document serving as receipt, contract evidence, and often document of title.
Dangerous goods handling follows IMDG Code with classification, segregation, labeling, and emergency preparedness.
Port security under ISPS Code requires risk assessment, access control, monitoring, and security plans for ships and facilities.
Port State Control inspections verify foreign vessels comply with safety, pollution-prevention, and labor conventions.
Pilotage is navigational assistance by licensed marine pilots for safe harbor entry, maneuvering, and departure.
Towage uses tugboats to assist vessel maneuvering, especially in constrained waters and adverse conditions.
VTS monitors and manages vessel movements to improve navigational safety and traffic efficiency.
Notices to mariners communicate navigation hazards, chart updates, and temporary restrictions.
Port dues are charges levied on vessels for use of port infrastructure and services.
Wharfage is a cargo-related fee charged for use of wharf or terminal facilities.
Storage charges apply when cargo remains in terminal beyond free-time period.
Free time is allowed period for cargo stay in terminal without storage penalties.
Line-haul transport is the long-distance movement segment between major logistics nodes.
Last-mile logistics is the final delivery leg from terminal or depot to consignee site.
A port master plan defines long-term demand, layout, phased infrastructure, connectivity, and environmental strategy.
Phased development sequences investments over time to match demand growth and reduce overcapacity risk.
Capacity planning estimates infrastructure and equipment required to meet target service levels and forecast demand.
Demand forecast projects future cargo volumes, vessel calls, and cargo mix to support planning decisions.
Design vessel is representative ship size and characteristics used as baseline for geometric and structural design.
Return period is average interval between events exceeding a specified magnitude, used in design criteria.
Storm surge is abnormal sea-level rise generated by atmospheric pressure and wind during severe storms.
HAT is highest predictable tide level under average meteorological conditions.
LAT is lowest predictable tide level and often reference for charted depths.
MSL is long-term average sea level used as vertical reference in engineering and hydrography.
Longshore transport is sediment movement parallel to shoreline driven by oblique wave action.
Littoral drift is alongshore sediment transport process affecting coastal morphology and harbor shoaling.
Sedimentation is deposition of suspended particles that can reduce channel and basin depth over time.
Capital dredging creates initial project depth and width for channels, basins, and berths.
Maintenance dredging restores depth lost to sedimentation and is a recurring operational requirement.
Dredged material management covers characterization, transport, beneficial use, and compliant disposal of dredged sediments.
Beneficial use places dredged sediments in constructive applications such as reclamation, wetlands, or shoreline nourishment.
Reclamation creates new land from sea or tidal areas using fill and perimeter protection structures.
Ground improvement increases soil strength and reduces settlement for port foundations on weak ground.
Liquefaction is temporary loss of soil strength during cyclic loading, especially in saturated loose sands.
Settlement is downward movement of ground or structure over time due to loading and soil consolidation.
Marine corrosion degrades steel and reinforced concrete in chloride-rich environments, requiring protective systems.
Cathodic protection reduces metal corrosion by shifting electrochemical potential using sacrificial anodes or impressed current.
Life-cycle cost includes initial CAPEX plus OPEX, maintenance, renewal, and residual value over project life.
CAPEX is spending on long-term infrastructure and equipment assets.
OPEX is recurring operating and maintenance spending required for daily terminal and marine operations.
Port decarbonization reduces greenhouse gas emissions through cleaner fuels, electrification, efficiency, and modal optimization.
Shore power supplies electricity from land to berthed ships so onboard engines can be turned off.
Ballast water management controls invasive species transfer and follows IMO Ballast Water Management Convention requirements.
MARPOL is the main international convention for prevention of pollution from ships.
IMO is the UN specialized agency that develops global regulations for shipping safety, security, and environmental performance.
PIANC is an international technical association that publishes guidance for waterborne transport infrastructure.
